title: Calcolatore Ovulazione
description: Calcola i giorni fertili e la data di ovulazione in base al tuo ciclo mestruale. Pianifica la gravidanza o evita giorni fertili.
ymyl_disclaimer: This calculator provides estimates based on a standard ovulation model. Individual cycles vary. This tool is not a substitute for medical advice or contraception. Consult a healthcare provider for fertility planning.

faq.title: Domande Frequenti
faq.q1: Come viene calcolata la data di ovulazione?
faq.a1: L'ovulazione avviene tipicamente 14 giorni prima dell'inizio del ciclo successivo (non 14 giorni dopo l'inizio dell'UPM). Per un ciclo di 28 giorni: ovulazione al giorno 14. Per un ciclo di 35 giorni: ovulazione al giorno 21. Il calcolo preciso è: Data UPM + (Durata ciclo − 14). La finestra fertile è 5 giorni prima e 1-2 giorni dopo l'ovulazione.
faq.a2: La finestra fertile è il periodo di massima probabilità di concepimento: 5 giorni prima dell'ovulazione più il giorno dell'ovulazione stesso (6 giorni totali). Gli spermatozoi sopravvivono fino a 5 giorni nell'apparato riproduttivo femminile; l'ovocita è fertile per 12-24 ore dopo l'ovulazione. La probabilità massima è nei 2 giorni precedenti l'ovulazione.
faq.a3: Metodi per confermare l'ovulazione: test di ovulazione LH (rilevano il picco dell'ormone luteinizzante 24-36 ore prima dell'ovulazione), monitoraggio della temperatura basale corporea (TBC — aumenta di 0,2-0,5°C dopo l'ovulazione), muco cervicale (diventa filante e trasparente come albume d'uovo nei giorni fertili), ecografia monitoraggio follicolare.
faq.q4: Questo calcolatore è affidabile per la pianificazione della famiglia?
faq.a4: Il calcolatore fornisce stime basate sul ciclo medio. L'accuratezza dipende dalla regolarità del ciclo. Per cicli irregolari, le stime sono meno affidabili. Non è un metodo contraccettivo sicuro — l'efficacia del metodo del ritmo è circa 76-88% con uso tipico, molto inferiore ai metodi contraccettivi moderni.
faq.q5: Cosa influenza la data di ovulazione?
faq.a5: Stress, malattia, variazioni di peso significative, intensa attività fisica, farmaci (es. anovulatori, antibiotici), disturbi ormonali (PCOS, ipotiroidismo) possono ritardare o sopprimere l'ovulazione. Anche i viaggi attraverso fusi orari possono influenzare temporaneamente il ciclo.
faq.q6: Cosa è la PCOS e come influenza l'ovulazione?
faq.a6: La sindrome dell'ovaio policistico (PCOS) è una condizione ormonale che colpisce il 5-10% delle donne in età fertile. Causa cicli irregolari o assenti, ovulazione infrequente o assente, e può rendere difficile il concepimento. Le donne con PCOS spesso hanno cicli molto variabili — un calcolatore basato sul ciclo medio è meno affidabile in questi casi.
faq.q7: Quando è il momento migliore per fare un test di gravidanza?
faq.a7: Il test di gravidanza più accurato si fa dal primo giorno di ritardo del ciclo — ovvero circa 14 giorni dopo l'ovulazione. Test sensibili possono rilevare la gravidanza 3-4 giorni prima del ciclo atteso. Farlo troppo presto aumenta il rischio di falso negativo perché i livelli di hCG potrebbero non essere ancora sufficienti.
faq.q8: Come tengo traccia del mio ciclo mestruale?
faq.a8: Registra: data di inizio di ogni ciclo, durata del ciclo, caratteristiche del muco cervicale, temperatura basale corporea (opzionale). App specifiche (Clue, Flo, Natural Cycles) facilitano il monitoraggio e migliorano le previsioni nel tempo. Dopo 3-6 mesi di dati, le previsioni diventano più precise per i tuoi cicli specifici.

content.whatIs:
The ovulation calculator predicts your ovulation date, fertile window, and next period date based on the first day of your last menstrual period and your average cycle length. It applies the standard reproductive endocrinology model — ovulation occurs 14 days before the next period, with a fertile window extending from 5 days before to 1 day after — to give you the most likely window for natural conception or for family planning awareness.

Understanding your cycle is foundational to reproductive health whether you are trying to conceive, avoiding pregnancy, or simply gaining awareness of your own biology. The menstrual cycle is divided into two phases: the follicular phase (from menstruation through ovulation) and the luteal phase (from ovulation to the next menstruation). The luteal phase is remarkably consistent at approximately 14 days in most women, regardless of cycle length. This means that cycle length variation — whether you have a 24-day or a 35-day cycle — comes almost entirely from differences in the follicular phase, not the luteal phase. This is the biological basis for this calculator's formula.

This calculator provides calendar-based estimates that are most accurate for women with regular cycles. Real fertility awareness benefits from combining calendar prediction with physical signs of ovulation (cervical mucus changes, basal body temperature) and LH surge testing for maximum accuracy. Think of the dates provided here as a starting framework — the true fertile window is confirmed by combining multiple observations.
content.formula:
Ovulation Day in Cycle = Cycle Length − 14
Ovulation Date = Last Period Date + Ovulation Day

Fertile Window:
  Start = Ovulation Date − 5 days  (sperm survival)
  End   = Ovulation Date + 1 day   (egg viability)
  Peak fertility days = Ovulation Date − 2 through Ovulation Date

Next Period Date = Last Period Date + Cycle Length

Days Until Ovulation = Ovulation Date − Today
  (negative = ovulation has already passed this cycle)

Example cycle lengths and ovulation days:
  24-day cycle → ovulation on day 10
  26-day cycle → ovulation on day 12
  28-day cycle → ovulation on day 14 (standard)
  30-day cycle → ovulation on day 16
  32-day cycle → ovulation on day 18
  35-day cycle → ovulation on day 21
content.example:
Example 1 — Standard 28-day cycle:
Last period: January 1, 2025
Ovulation day: 28 − 14 = day 14 → January 15, 2025
Fertile window: January 10–16, 2025
Next period: January 29, 2025
Days until ovulation (from Jan 10): 5 days
Peak fertility (highest conception chance): Jan 13–15

Example 2 — Longer 32-day cycle:
Last period: January 1, 2025
Ovulation day: 32 − 14 = day 18 → January 19, 2025
Fertile window: January 14–20, 2025
Next period: February 2, 2025

Example 3 — Short 24-day cycle:
Last period: January 1, 2025
Ovulation day: 24 − 14 = day 10 → January 11, 2025
Fertile window: January 6–12, 2025
Note: period bleeding may still be present at start of fertile window.
content.howItWorks_1: Enter the date of the first day of your last menstrual period — the first day of actual flow, not spotting. This is the anchor date for all calculations. If you are not sure of the exact date, use the best available date from your period tracking or calendar.
content.howItWorks_2: Enter your average cycle length in days. A menstrual cycle is counted from the first day of one period to the day before the next period begins. Typical cycle lengths range from 21 to 35 days, with 28 days being the average used in medical literature. If your cycles vary significantly, use the average over your last 3–6 cycles. Avoid using a single unusually short or long cycle as your baseline.
content.howItWorks_3: Click Calculate to see your estimated ovulation date, complete fertile window (start and end dates), next expected period date, and how many days until ovulation from today. If the days-until-ovulation number is negative, ovulation is estimated to have already passed this cycle — the results still show the dates for this cycle, and the next period date tells you when your new cycle begins.
content.whyItMatters:
Fertility awareness — understanding when ovulation occurs — is one of the most practically useful pieces of reproductive health knowledge available to people with cycles. For those trying to conceive, accurately identifying the fertile window significantly reduces the time to conception. Research shows that couples who time intercourse to the fertile window (particularly the 2–3 days before and including ovulation) have conception rates 2–3× higher per cycle compared to random timing. Given that the average fertile window is only 6 days per cycle, and most of a cycle is infertile, random timing has limited efficiency.

The psychological benefits of fertility awareness are also significant. For the approximately 1 in 8 couples who experience difficulty conceiving, having a clear understanding of the cycle can reduce anxiety, prevent misattribution of infertility (e.g., blaming physiology when the issue was timing), and guide more productive conversations with reproductive health providers. Many couples seeking infertility consultations are not aware that human conception is only possible on 6 days per cycle, and some have been having intercourse well outside their fertile window entirely.

Beyond conception, cycle awareness has broader health applications. Tracking cycle regularity allows early detection of cycle changes that may indicate hormonal imbalances, thyroid disorders, PCOS, or perimenopause. The menstrual cycle is increasingly recognized by endocrinologists as a 'fifth vital sign' — a regular, predictable biological process whose disruption signals potential systemic health issues. A woman who knows her normal cycle length of 28 days can quickly identify when a 35-day or 42-day cycle is abnormal and merits investigation.
content.limitations:
The calendar method used by this calculator assumes a consistent luteal phase of exactly 14 days. In reality, the luteal phase ranges from 10 to 16 days in healthy women, with an average of 14 days. A shorter-than-typical luteal phase (luteal phase defect, fewer than 10 days) can affect fertility and is not detectable by this calculator. Additionally, the follicular phase varies not just between women but in the same woman cycle to cycle — stress, illness, travel, weight changes, and significant exercise can all shift ovulation timing by several days.

This calculator does not adjust for: irregular cycles, absent ovulation (anovulation), recent discontinuation of hormonal contraception (which can take 2–3 months for cycles to normalize), breastfeeding (which often suppresses ovulation), thyroid disorders, or PCOS. For all of these conditions, calendar-based prediction is substantially less reliable than for women with regular cycles, and clinical fertility monitoring is recommended.

This tool is not a medical device and should not replace clinical evaluation for anyone experiencing infertility. If you have been trying to conceive for 12 months without success (6 months if over age 35), referral to a reproductive endocrinologist is the standard recommendation. Testing for both partners (semen analysis for male, hormonal assessment and cycle monitoring for female) provides the most complete picture and allows targeted intervention.
content.tips_1: For the highest conception probability, plan intercourse on the 2–3 days leading up to and including the estimated ovulation date — not after. Sperm survive up to 5 days in fertile cervical mucus, so early-in-the-window timing is highly effective. The egg is viable for only 12–24 hours after ovulation — waiting until after ovulation to time intercourse significantly reduces the probability per cycle.
content.tips_2: Combine this calendar estimate with ovulation predictor kit (OPK) testing for significantly higher accuracy. Begin testing urine 2 days before the estimated ovulation date (in the early afternoon, not morning — LH peaks later in the day). A positive OPK (test line as dark or darker than control) indicates the LH surge and predicts ovulation within 12–36 hours. This real-time biological confirmation eliminates calendar estimation error entirely.
content.tips_3: Track cervical mucus changes as an additional free, natural fertility sign. In the days before ovulation, discharge changes from dry or sticky (infertile) to creamy, then to clear, stretchy, and slippery (fertile). This 'egg white cervical mucus' (EWCM) is the most visible sign that the fertile window is open and the body is approaching ovulation. Observing EWCM combined with this calculator's prediction and OPK testing creates a three-layer confirmation of peak fertility.
content.tips_4: If you have irregular cycles or have been trying to conceive for 3+ months without success, see a gynecologist or reproductive endocrinologist before further calendar-based attempts. Blood tests on day 3 (FSH, LH, estradiol, AMH) assess ovarian reserve. A day-21 progesterone test confirms whether ovulation occurred. Cycle monitoring via transvaginal ultrasound can directly visualize follicle development and confirm ovulation with certainty — information no calculator can provide.
